segment-text: MANCHESTER-BY-THE-SEA
MANCHESTER-BY-THE-SEA REVISIONS 3/10/15
By Kenneth Lonergan
EXT. BOSTON -- ROXBURY -- APARTMENT HOUSE. DAY.

It's a cold winter day on a narrow street. In front of a small apartment building, LEE CHANDLER the custodian, sweeps away the old snow on the pavement, then sprinkles salt in front of the building. He is 40, wearing janitor's coveralls under his weatherbeaten winter jacket.
INT. BASEMENT. DAY.
> He organizes the trash cans and recycling in the basement. > He bleeds the boiler of rusty sludge.
INT. APARTMENT BUILDING HALLWAY. DAY.
He vacuums the hall with an industrial vacuum cleaner on a fifty foot yellow extension cord.
INT. MRS GROOM'S APARTMENT. DAY.
Standing on a ladder in a small grandmotherly apartment, Lee changes a light bulb in the very small bathroom. MRS GROOM, 70s, is on the phone outside the open bathroom door.
EXT. ROXBURY -- ANOTHER BUILDING. DAY. A different apartment building on a similar street. INT. BATHROOM. DAY.
Lee works on a leaky toilet while MR MARTINEZ, 50s, a big man in an undershirt and glasses, stands by watching.
I don't know why the hell it keeps dripping. All night long, drip, drip. I've had the fucking thing repaired ten times.
You need a new stopper.
Oh is that it?
See how it's rotted around the edges? It doesn't make a seal, so
the water drips into the bowl. I
replacing the whole apparatus.
What do you recommend?
Lee starts putting away his tools and cleaning up.
Well, I could replace the stopper first, and if that doesn't work, then I would come back and replace the whole apparatus.
But you don't have a professional recommendation?
It's really up to you.
Well, tomorrow I got my sister-inlaw coming over with my nephews ...and I gotta take my car in...
Lee waits while Mr Martinez works out his schedule.
EXT. ROXBURY -- A SLIGHTLY MORE UPSCALE STREET. DUSK. A marginally more upscale building. INT. BATHROOM. DUSK.
Lee looks down at a stopped-up toilet. Behind him is MARIANNE, slender, 30s, attractive, wearing everyday aroundthe-house clothes. 3.
I am so sorry. This is so gross.
It's all right.
He plunges her toilet carefully and methodically. LATER -- He wipes up the floor. Marianne comes in.
Oh Lee, you don't have to do that, honestly. That's OK.
Well -- God. Thank you so much, I am so sorry.
LATER -- He is washing his hands in her bathroom sink. IN HER SMALL LIVING ROOM -- He comes out of the bathroom. Marianne is now dressed up to go out. She looks great. All set.
Thank you so much. Can I give you a tip?
You mean, like a suggestion?
(Taking out a ten) No -- I mean -- like, a tip...
That's all right. Have a good night.
Oh, please. I'd feel bad.
(Takes the money) OK, thanks a lot. Good night.
Good night! And thank you so much.
INT. MRS OLSEN'S BATHROOM. DAY.
Lee is down on his hand and knees. MRS OLSEN, 40s, in a bathrobe, is very good-looking but bad-tempered and nervous. She's in a bathrobe.
How many times do we have to fix these fucking pipes? Every time I take a shower their entire apartment has a flood. It's driving me insane.

I'll bring the plumber tomorrow but I'd say we're gonna have to break through the tile and try to isolate the leak, because there was quite a lot of water -MRS OLSEN But how do you know it's me? Why is it automatically me?
Because it if was coming from higher up you'd have water damage on the ceiling too, and maybe in your wall, and it's all dry. Great.
MRS OLSEN
Lee looks at the bathtub.
It might just be the caulking. This whole tub needs to be re-caulked. Did you take a bath or shower in the last couple of hours? Yes...
Well, it could actually just be that.
OK. And how are you planning to find that out?
Well, we could turn on the shower and see if it drip downstairs...
You want me to take a shower now?
You want me to take a shower while
you stand there watching, to see if the water drips down into the Friedrich's apartment?
I don't really give a fuck what you do, Mrs. Olsen. I just want to find the leak.
Mrs Olsen goes white with shock and fury.
No, you can get out of my apartment and don't ever come back.
How dare you fucking talk to me like that? Get the fuck out of my house before I call the police!
You're blockin' the doorway.
INT. MR EMERY'S OFFICE. NIGHT.
The building manager's office. MR EMERY is in his 50s. Lee sits in the chair before the crowded desk.
What the fuck's matter with you? You can't talk to the tenants like that!
Lee shrugs.
Look, Lee. You do a good job. You're dependable. But I get these complaints all the time. You're rude, you're unfriendly, you don't say good mornin'. I mean come on!

Mr Emery, I fix the plumbing. I take out the garbage. I paint their apartments. I do electrical work -which we both know is against the law. I show up on time, I'm workin' four buildings and you get all the money. So do whatever you're gonna do.
Would you be willin' to apologize to Mrs Olsen? For what?
For callin' her a cunt...!
That's not what I called her.
All right, all right, I'll talk to her.
Lee gets up to go.
INT. A LOUD ROXBURY BAR. NIGHT.
> Lee drinks alone at a small, crowded neighborhood bar. > AT THE BAR -- Lee is waiting for service. Someone bumps a CUTE GIRL, 30s, into him. She spills some beer on him.
That's OK. I'm OK...
Well, now I spilled beer all over you, my name's Sharon. That's OK.
And you are... Lee.

She gets the message that he is not interested. He pats himself dry, not looking at her. The bar is far less packed. We see SHARON across the room, talking to a girlfriend. At the bar, Lee is drinking alone. He's pretty drunk by now. He is looking at two BUSINESSMEN, 40s. One of them notices and mentions it to the other. They look at him for a moment then ignore him. He gets up and walks toward them. They are surprised at his approach.
Good. I'm good. Can I ask you guys, have we met before?
The two men look at each other then back at Lee
No.
I don't think so either.
So you guys don't know me? No, Yeah. No. Do we?
Well then what the fuck are you lookin' at me for? Excuse me?
I said why the fuck are you lookin' at me?
Sir, we really weren't looking at you -BARTENDER (Hurrying over) Hey, Lee...Lee...!
Hey! Take a fuckin' walk. Hey -- Paul -- No -- don't apologize to this asshole-(To LEE) Take a hike!

Lee HITS the 2nd Businessman and knocks him into a wall. Several pictures fall and smash on the floor. Oh, Goddamn it -Lee punches the 1ST BUSINESSMAN's nose. He falls back and grabs his face, blood streaming from both nostrils. The 2nd Businessmen and Lee swipe at each other.
You broke my fuckin' nose!
Goddamn lunatic --
The BARTENDER leaps over the bar and grabs Lee from behind -Other guys join in to break it up.
Lee! Lee! Lee! Enough!
Break it up! Break it up!
Lemme go. I gotta go take a hike. General melee.
INT. LEE'S BASEMENT APARTMENT. NIGHT.

Lee turns the light on and comes in. He is a little roughed up from the fight. At his dresser, Lee pulls on sweatpants and an undershirt. There are THREE FRAMED PHOTOS in imitation silver frames standing on the little dresser. We don't see the photos. Lee sits on the sofa with a beer and turns the TV on to a late-night Sports program. Slowly he falls asleep. The can in his hand tips slowly sideways and spills onto the sofa.
EXT. LEE'S STREET. DAY.
It's snowing. Lots of slow, heavy flakes, very pretty.
EXT. LEE'S BUILDING - WINTER. DAY.
Lee is shoveling snow. The air is clear and cold. The whole street is beautified by the recent snow storm. His iPhone rings. He takes off his gloves. Digs out the phone.
Hello ... This is Lee ... Oh ... When did that happen? ... Well, how is he? ... OK. Uh...No. Don't do that. I'll come up right now ... OK. Thank you.
He hangs up and goes inside with the shovel, leaving the snow before the building only partially cleared and salted down.
INT. LEE'S CAR (MOVING). DAY.
Lee sits behind the wheel, trying to get out of Boston and onto Rt 1. North. He's talking on his iPhone.

(Into his iPhone) Mr Emery, it's Lee again. I contacted Jose, who says he can cover for me til Friday night at least, and then Gene MacAdavey can take over till I get back. I'll be in Manchester at least a week or two. I'll call again when I have more information. Goodbye.
He hangs up and drives into increasingly heavy traffic.
Come on, come on.
The traffic slows. He becomes increasingly anxious.
EXT. RT. 128 -- LEE'S CAR (MOVING). DAY.
Lee's car takes the exit for North Salem.
EXT. NORTH SALEM HOSPITAL. DAY.
Lee drives through the grounds of a big modern hospital. He knows exactly where he's going. He parks and gets out. He walks quickly to the main entrance, then breaks into a run.
INT. NORTH SALEM HOSPITAL. DAY.
We lead/follow Lee as he walks quickly through the halls toward the ICU, navigating the twists and turns from habit. He goes into the ICU -21
INT. ICU -- NURSE'S STATION -- CONTINUOUS. DAY.
-- and approaches GEORGE, around 50, a big weatherbeaten guy, and NURSE IRENE, 40s. They both react as Lee approaches. Hiya, Lee.
Is he dead?
George's eyes fill with tears. He makes a helpless gesture.
I'm sorry, Lee. He passed away about an hour ago. Oh.
I'm so sorry.
Lee looks at the floor, hands on his hips. Nurse Irene gives his arm an awkward squeeze. Lee stares into the middle distance for a moment.
Did you see him?
Yeah. I mean -- No --
George br --
I brought him in.
...We were lookin' at the boat this mornin', and he just -- I don't know, he just, like, fell over. I thought he was kiddin' me at first. Then I called the ambulance...and uh -- that was it.
Lee shakes his head, still staring at the floor.
I'll just call Dr Muller and tell him that you're here.
Where's Dr Betheny?
Oh, she's on maternity leave. Oh here he is. DR MULLER, 40s, has just joined them.
Lee? I'm Dr Muller. We spoke on the phone. Yeah. Hi.
I'm very, very sorry. Thank you.
Hello, George.
He shake hands with George.
How you holding up?
Oh -- Great! You know.
Well...it's a very sad day. Yeah.
George starts to cry. He looks down and wipes his eyes.
Where's my brother?
He's downstairs. You can see him if you want.
What happened?

Well, you know his heart was very weak at this point, and it just gave out. If it's any comfort, I don't think he suffered very much. I'm sorry you didn't get here in time, but as I told you on the phone --
Aw, fuck this. (He looks at the floor. Long Pause. He looks up.) Sorry.
That's perfectly all right.
That's OK, buddy.
Did anybody call my Uncle?
DR MULLER and GEORGE glance at each other.
Their Uncle Donny.
Yeah, my Aunt and Uncle. Somebody shoulda called them. What?
No, Lee -- Lee, no --
-- Donny got a job in Minnesota, like -Minnesota?
No.
-- awhile ago. Yeah: He got a job with some outfit in Minnetonka, Minnesota, if you can believe that. Joe didn't tell you about that?
I can call 'em if you want, Lee. And tell 'em what happened.
OK. Thanks...
Tell 'em...Tell 'em what happened. Tell 'em I'll call 'em tonight, probably tomorrow. Talk about arrangements.
Oh, no problem -*
Sure, I can do that.
And somebody better call my wife.
There is a confused, embarrassed hesitation.
Ex-wife. Yes. Sorry. I meant Randi.
You mean Randi? That's OK -- I already thought of that. I'll take care of it.
OK, thanks.
Can I see him now? Sure.
Lee -- I can wait up here, Lee, in case you need anything. OK.
Dr Muller leads Lee away. George breaks down again. GEORGE
Would you like a Kleenex?
Thanks. Yeah. I'm sorry.
INT. HOSPITAL ELEVATOR.
Dr Muller and Lee ride down very slowly.
How is Dr Betheny?
Oh, she's doing very well. She just had twin girls.
Oh yeah. Irene told me.
Apparently weigh about eleven pounds apiece. So she's gonna have her hands full for a while...I'll call her this afternoon and tell her what happened.
She was very good to him.
Yes she was. EIGHT YEARS AGO --
INT. JOE CHANDLER'S HOSPITAL ROOM. DAY.

JOE CHANDLER, Lee's older brother by five years, is lying in the hospital bed. There's a close resemblance between them. ELISE, Joe's wife, the same age as Joe, pretty, anxious and high-strung -- stands near to STANLEY CHANDLER -- Lee and Joe's father, 70s. He sits in one chair. LEE sits in another. They are all listening to DR BETHENY, 30s. She is small, intense, very serious and focused and level-headed, but thoroughly well-meaning and decent. The bed area is curtained off from the other patients in the room.
The disease is commonly referred to as congestive heart failure -Oh my God!
Are you familiar with it? No...!
Then what are you sayin' "Oh my God" for?
Because what is it?
She's tryin' to explain it to us, honey. I'm sorry, Dr Beth...uh...
I'm sorry. I can never get it right.
Don't worry about it. Not a problem.
So, you were saying, Dr Beth. JOE It's Dr Betheny, Dad.
Dr Betheny, Daddy, Try to get it right..
It's a comedy routine!
Would you let her tell it?
Elise, please...
Oh my God: When am I gonna put one foot right?
Honey, for Christ's sakes!
How about a hint?
Stanley takes Elise's hand and holds onto it.
Elise...Sweetheart...Let's just let her explain the situation to us... Daddy...
What? She's fine. We're all upset. We're all gonna listen, then we're gonna ask everything we wanna ask, and then we're gonna figure out what do to, together. Right? Right.
INT. WAITING ROOM. DAY -- SIMULTANEOUS
PATRICK CHANDLER, 7, is watching TV, with an 18 YEAR-OLD BABYSITTER. Two WOMEN VISITORS are chatting as he watches.
It's a gradual deterioration of the muscles of the heart. It's usually
INT. HOSPITAL - JOE'S ROOM. CONTINUOUS.

...Some people can live as long as fifty or sixty years with just an occasional attack. But most people suffer periodic episodes, like the one you had on Monday, which mimic the symptoms of a heart attack and which further weaken the muscle. They can put you out of commission for a week, two weeks. (MORE)
And you'll need to be hospitalized so we can monitor your heart, because the risk of cardiac arrest is elevated for a week or two. Oh my God.
(Pats her hand) OK...OK...
But in between these episodes, most people feel perfectly healthy and you can basically live a normal life.
So...What do you mean that some people live as long as fifty or sixty years? You mean total? Or from when they're diagnosed with this, or what?
Everyone is stunned into silence, even Elise.
For approximately eighty percent of patients your age the most common statistical life expectancy is five years or less.
Elise grips Stan's hand. Lee looks at the floor.
But the statistics vary widely, and they're just statistics. You're not a statistic, you're just one person, and we don't know what's going to happen to you yet. But it's not a good disease.
What's a good disease?
Poison Ivy.
(Rising) I do not see where the humor lies in this situation.
Elise, you must calm down.
Honey, please...
I'm sorry. I'm really not trying to --
(To Dr Betheny) Don't -- it's fine.
Elise pulls her hand away from Stan and waves "No" at them.
No. No more -- I'm not gonna -STAN Elise, let's get you a glass of water --
No m -- No.
Daddy. Forget it.
Hey, shut up.
Yeah, forget it. Forget it like you -- No, you know what? I'm tired of bein' the bad guy here.
Jesus Christ! Who's in the fuckin' hospital?! Nobody th --
Right! So I'll be the bad guy, and you be in the hospital and explain the jokes to your son. I'm goin' home.
You're goin' home.
Sweetheart --
SHE WALKS OUT fast, her heels clicking against the floor.
Lemme get her back. Fuck her.
You wanna stop with that shit?
Come on with that stuff already!
INT. HOSPITAL HALL & WAITING ROOM. DAY -- CONTINUOUS.

The Baby-Sitter and 7-Year Old Patrick see Elise coming down the hall. The Baby-Sitter comes to the waiting room door. BABYSITTER Mrs Chandler? I think someone could use some atten -Elise walks past her. The BABYSITTER sits back down again next to 7-YEAR OLD PATRICK.
INT. HOSPITAL ELEVATOR/LOWER LEVEL HALLWAY.
The ELEVATOR DOOR OPENS AT LL2. Dr Muller and Lee come out.
INT. MORGUE.
A SECURITY GUARD opens the door for DR MULLER and LEE.
Thank you, Tony.
Lee goes in and looks down at the body. Pause. OK.
(Hesitates)
Take your time.
Lee moves closer to the body. He touches Joe's hand. It feels dead so he touches his shoulder where there's a sleeve.
EXT. THE SEA - JOE'S BOAT. DAY.

Autumn. LEE, JOE and 8 YEAR-OLD PATRICK are on JOE'S BOAT. The Cape Ann coast is in the distance. The boat is rigged for whale-watching and deep sea fishing charters. Lee discreetly keeps a hand near the rod as 8 Year-Old Patrick trolls. Joe is at the tiller. He's looking thinner but better. Like that? 8 YEAR-OLD PATRICK
Yeah, only keep your thumb off the line, 'cause if you get a strike it's gonna slice it right open. And you know what happens then. What?
8 YEAR-OLD PATRICK
The sharks are gonna smell that blood and rip this boat apart.
No they won't. Dad, will they?
Oh yeah. I seen a school of sharks tear a boat to pieces like it was made of cardboard, just 'cause some kid threw a band-aid in the water.
No you didn't.
Sometimes the only way to keep 'em off is to throw the kid directly in the ocean to distract 'em.
Shut up. Sharks don't even swim in schools.
Is this kid smart or what?
Yep. And a really smart kid is exactly the kind of quality meal a humongous school of sharks is lookin' for when they're circlin' a boat.
Uncle Lee! Shut up!
Patrick's REEL starts SPINNING OUT with a thrilling whine.
Strike! Strike! Ease up on the drag -And watch that fuckin' thumb!
Look out, look out! You got a strike! Ease up, ease up!
(Hits him) No swearing!
Patrick loses his balance. Lee catches him and props him up.
Don't hit me -- ! Catch the fish! We're doin' fine. (To Joe) Just drive the boat. Patty, pull up sharp! Come on, buddy! There you go! (To JOE) Mind your business!
What are you guys doin'? Hook the fish! Get the hook in him before he -- ! I'm drivin' the Goddamn boat. Get that hook in him!
Lee helps Patrick pull the rod back sharply a few times.
What kind of fish is it?
Gotta be a Great White, Patty -- Maybe a Baracudda -Shut up!
Feels like a Great White Shark to me.
Patrick is ecstatic with nerves and excitement.
INT. MORGUE. DAY
Lee looks at Joe's body. He leans over and kisses his cheek. He embraces the body as best he can. Dr Muller drops back discreetly. Lee walks out past Dr Muller. Dr Muller follows.
(To the Security Guard) Thanks, Tony.
INT. ELEVATOR.
Lee and Dr Muller ride up again in silence. We hear the O.C. sound of a ROARING FIRE: Something enormous, like a forest fire. The sound cuts off as -32
INT. BEVERLY HOSPITAL. ICU. FLOOR. DAY - CONTINUOUS.
The ELEVATOR DOOR OPENS. Lee and Dr Muller come out and walk to the Nurse's Station, where IRENE and GEORGE wait.
I gotta get up to Manchester. Nobody told Patrick, right?
No -- you had asked us to wait for you to get here -LEE (On "us") Yes -- Thank you. So...What is the procedure now?
Well -- You should make arrangements with a funeral parlor, and they pretty much take care of everything.
I don't know the name of one.
We can help you with that. Yeah.
And they come up and get him? Yes. Yes.
NURSE IRENE
I'll make those calls, Lee -GEORGE (CONT'D) Lemme know if you need any help with anything.
OK. And -- OK. Thanks. And -Yeah. Plus I gotta call you about the boat, and the web site. All that shit.
Sure. I'm around.
OK. I better get up there before school lets out.
You just have to sign for Joe's belongings.

INSERT -- Lee signs a form. Nurse Irene puts a labeled plastic ziplock bag containing the contents of Joe's pockets on the nurse's desk. Inside are Joe's cell phone, wallet, keys, an old Swiss Army knife, and a plastic pill-case.
INT. LEE'S CAR/HOSPITAL PARKING LOT. DAY.

Lee drops the plastic bag on the other seat and starts the car. A CELL PHONE RINGS. Lee is momentarily disoriented, then realizes it's not his. JOE'S PHONE is RINGING inside the plastic bag. It says "PATRICK." Lee turns off the phone and puts the bag in the glove compartment.
INT./EXT. LEE'S CAR (MOVING)/RT. 128 NORTH. DAY.
Lee drives up the highway.
EXT. THE OCEAN - MANCHESTER-BY-THE-SEA. DAY.

FROM THE OCEAN -- We see the wealthy summer resort clinging to the Cape Ann winter coastline. The sound of the OCEAN continues over the following driving scenes, shifting partway into the ROARING FIRE SOUND...
INT. LEE'S CAR (MOVING) DAY.
Thru the windshield, Lee sees the MANCHESTER EXIT approach. He takes the exit.
INT./EXT. LEE'S CAR/MANCHESTER. DAY.
Lee drives past the old houses of the little town.
INT. LEE'S MANCHESTER HOUSE. DAY. Evening of the same day as the fishing scene. Lee comes into
his small house and takes off his wet things. In the living room, his daughter SUZY, 7, is watching TV. A fire is crackling behind a firescreen. A radio is playing O.S.
Hi honey! (To the girl) Hi, Suzy. Daddy's home. (Pause) Hi, Suzy. Daddy's home. Hi Daddy.
Hi, sweetheart.
He bends down to kiss her. She hooks her arm around his neck and pulls him off balance, her eyes locked on the TV screen. Hug.
Jesus Christ, you're breakin' my neck.
He kisses her and she releases him. Lee?
Yeah, hiya!
INT. BEDROOM. DAY.

RANDI, Lee's wife, 30s, is in bed with a cold. She is tough, loving and sarcastic. The room is littered with Kleenex and cold remedies and clothes. KAREN, 5, is playing with colored plastic blocks on the floor. There is a CRIB in a corner. Hi honey.
You have a good time?
Yeah, really good. Where's your mother? They just left. Oh no.
Yeah, she really missed you.
There's always next time. Did you sleep?
Oh, yeah. It's always restful when my mother's here.
That's too bad. Hi Kary. Hi Daddy.
(Picking her up) Hello sweetheart.
Put me down.
I'm puttin' you down. Relax.
He kisses her and starts to put her down. She wriggles and jerks unexpectedly backward. Lee almost loses his grip.
Hey, hey, hey!
Karen...!
(Puttng her down) Honey, don't jerk around like that, I almost dropped you on your head.
I'm making a hair salon.
Oh yeah? It's really good. You wanna cut my hair?
It's just for girls. I'm sorry.
I understand. (To Randi) How you feelin'?
Little better.
You sound better.
Did you actually use the fishin' tackle?
Yeah, we really did. Patrick got a humongous bluefish. 18 pounds.
Oh yeah? That's awesome!
I never seen anybody so happy in my life.
He crosses to her through the tissue-strewn, cluttered room.
It's nice in here. You keep it nice. What a homemaker. Fuck off.
He tries to kiss her. She turns her head.
Get away from me.
They kiss. She claps his hand onto her breast.
No, don't, stop, I'm sick.
They kiss some more. She shoves him away.
OK, get offa me.
Lee goes to the CRIB. Inside is STANLEY, 8 months old, awake and placid, waving his limbs at a multicolored mobile.
Hi Stanny. How come you're not cryin'?
Let him alone. He's been quiet for half an hour.
Lee picks the baby up.
Half an hour. What is that about? Take it easy. (To the baby) Hiya buddy. You are very handsome.
Oh Lee, please don't pick him up! If he's not makin' any noise, Leave Well Enough Alone.
"Leave well enough alone." That's what me and Mummy shoulda done instead of gettin' married.
(Opens her magazine) Just shut up.
...but then you wouldn't be here. And neither would your sisters. And I could watch the football game in my own livin' room. That's right, I could.
Go fuck yourself.
Lee kisses the baby and puts him back in the crib.
See? I didn't make him cry. 'Cause I know how to handle him.
How was Joe?
He's all right. You know? He's doin' all right.
And you didn't run outta beer? You got through the day OK?
Oh yeah. We were very temperate.
I don't know why you guys bother gettin' on the friggin' boat.
Because we love the sea.
I only had eight beers over a seven hour period. That's approximately one point four two somethin' beers per hour.
No, it's almost like a normal
person stayin' sober.
I told you I was cuttin' down.
Lee starts to get undressed.
What do you think you're gonna do?
I guess I'm gonna take a shower. Randi, I swear to God. You shoulda
seen the look on Patty's face when
She smiles at him. Lee crawls across the bed.
Get away. I'm sick. I'm deeply sick.
They kiss. Karen plays on the floor. The baby waves his arms. The TV can be heard from the other room.
He's not at school?
EXT./INT. MANCHESTER -- PINE STREET/LEE'S CAR. DAY.
Lee drives into town, talking on his cell phone.
I thought school let out at three o'clock -- What? I'm sorry. My cell phone -- what?
I'm pretty sure he -I'm pretty sure he woulda -That's all right. I'm pretty sure he woulda left for hockey practice by now.
EXT. MANCHESTER ESSEX REGIONAL HIGH SCHOOL. DAY
Over an establishing shot of the big school building we hear: He's on the hockey team?
INT. VICE PRINCIPLE'S OFFICE. DAY - CONTINUOUS.
PAUL, the Vice Principal, 40, is on the phone. His ASSISTANT, 50s, is on her computer nearby. WE CUT BETWEEN PAUL AND LEE.
Yeah, he's doin' real well, too. How's Joe doin'? He gonna be OK?
He's fine. Where's the practice at? The school?
No -- It's in Gloucester.
It's at the Gloucester -That's OK. Can you hear me? We play with the Rockport team. But they're the lead team.
It's not at school? What? I'm sorry -- This phone is -Yes. I understand --
OK, thanks, Paul. I gotta go.
Sure thing. Give Joe my regards, will you? I will.
INT. VICE PRINCIPAL'S OFFICE -- CONTINUOUS.
PAUL hangs up.
INT./EXT. LEE'S CAR/RT 128 NORTH. DAY.
Lee drives. The SIGN for GLOUCESTER and ROCKPORT is up ahead.
INT. GLOUCESTER MIDDLE SCHOOL - HOCKEY RINK. DAY.

The Rockport/Manchester team is having practice. PATRICK is on the ice. He is just 16, skinny, athletic, long-haired. He is bright, practical, pugnacious. The HOCKEY COACH, 40's, is shouting instructions. PATRICK checks another KID against the boards. They start fighting. They're evenly matched but Patrick is kind of wild. The COACH yanks Patrick off the other kid.
OK, break it up! Break it up! You! Chandler! One more time and you are OUT. Understand me?
Patrick sees LEE in the stands, over the coach's shoulder..
Aw, fuck me.
What's that, Chandler?
Aw, fuck my fuckin' ass.
OK, you are out! You're benched!
(To himself, skating away) Ask me if I give a shit.
What's that? What's the matter?
Patrick skates over to Lee. The Coach sees Lee and hesitates. A small scrappy kid named JOEL skates up, followed by C.J., a big handsome athletic kid. These are Patrick's friends.
That's his Uncle. His Dad must be in the hospital. Whose Dad? Chandler's? He's got congestive heart failure. Patrick's Dad, I mean. Not Patrick.
...He only comes up when Mr Chandler's in the hospital.
Some other kids skate up and are watching Patrick and LEE.
That's Lee Chandler? The Lee
Yeah, that story's bullshit.
You guys wanna watch the language? Sorry.
Sorry.
Across the rink, Lee is talking to Patrick. Patrick is kicking up little shards of ice with his skate. The COACH notices that all the kids have stopped to watch.
